{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tottori Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Nawa Jinja'''|名和神社}} | image = Nawa-jinja Rōmon.jpg | caption = Nawa Jinja in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|30|16|N|133|29|42|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nawa Shrine | image = [[File:Nawa-jinja Worship Hall.jpg|260px]]
Worship Hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=260}} | location = 556 Nawa, Daisen-chō, Saihaku-gun, Tottori-ken | coordinates = {{Coord|35|30|16|N|133|29|42|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | deity = Nawa Nagatoshi | Shrine rank = Bekkaku Kanpeisha • Beppyo Jinja | Main Building Style = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = [[May 7]] }} {{nihongo|'''Nawa Jinja'''|名和神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is celebrated for its [[cherry blossoms]].{{cite web |url=http://yokoso.pref.tottori.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=1873 |title=Nawa Jinja |publisher=[[Tottori Prefecture]] |accessdate=23 April 2011 |url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723211712/http://yokoso.pref.tottori.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=1873 |archivedate=23 July 2011 }} It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]], dedicated to the memory of [[Nawa Nagatoshi]]. ==Overview== '''Nawa Shrine''' (名和神社, Nawa Jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen-chō]], [[Tottori Prefecture]]. The shrine enshrines [[Nawa Nagatoshi]]. Its former shrine ranking under the modern system was that of a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The shrine's crest (kamon) is the "Ho Kagari Bune" of the Nawa clan. [[File:Nawa-jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Nawa-jinja Rōmon.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Rōmon gate]] == Deities == Nawa Shrine enshrines Nawa Nagatoshi as the main deity, along with 42 members of the Nawa clan. Nawa Nagatoshi was a samurai who supported Emperor Go-Daigo in overthrowing the Kamakura shogunate and fought for the Southern Court during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. * [[Nawa Yoshitaka]] - Eldest son of Nagatoshi. Killed in the Battle of Sakaiura in 1338. * [[Nawa Takamitsu]] - Third son of Nagatoshi. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Nawa Yasunaga - Younger brother of Nagatoshi (third brother). Committed suicide in Izumo Province in 1333. * Nawa Yukitomo - Younger brother of Nagatoshi (ninth brother). Committed suicide in Senjōzan, Hōki Province in 1334. * Nawa Nagashige - Nephew of Nagatoshi. * Nawa Yoshishige - Nephew of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Nagayoshi. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Takamichi - Nephew of Nagashige, second son of Suketaka. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takamasa - Younger brother of Takamichi. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Nagauji - Nephew of Nagatoshi, third son of Yukiuji. Killed in Hachiman in 1340. * Nawa Sadauji - Younger brother of Nagauji. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takakami - Nephew of Nagatoshi, second son of Takashige. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takanaga - Nephew of Nagatoshi, second son of Takakane. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Yukishige - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Yukimura. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Hidemura - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Yorimura. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Gorōbyōe-no-jō - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Koremura. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Shigemura - Younger brother of Gorōbyōe-no-jō. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Okimura - Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Nobusada - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Yukisada. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Hiromasa - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nobusada, second son of Hiromasa. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Hirotsugu - Grandson of Nobusada's cousin. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Sukesada - Cousin of Nagatoshi, younger brother of Nobusada. Killed in Nijo Domiya in 1333. * Nawa Sukeshige - Cousin of Nagatoshi, younger brother of Sukesada. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Nagashige - Eldest son of Nagasada. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Takanobu - Eldest son of Takayuki. Killed in Tomioka, Bizen Province in 1343. * Nawa Yukizane - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Yukitada. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Sukekuni - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Takasuke. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takakuni - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin, eldest son of Sukekuni. Killed in Sakainan, Echizen Province in 1336. * Uchikawa Masanobu - Retainer of Nagatoshi. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Masachika - Second son of Uchikawa Masanobu. Committed suicide at Mt. Shosha in Harima Province in 1334. * Uchikawa Masakazu - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, eldest son of Nagaaki. Killed in Nijo Domiya in 1333. * Uchikawa Masayuki - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, younger brother of Masakazu. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Yuki - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, younger brother of Masayuki. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Yoshihiro - Grandson of Uchikawa Masanobu, fourth son of Yoshimasa. Killed in Higo Province in 1378. * Uchikawa Yukikage - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Uchikawa Takekage - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Uchikawa Kunitoki - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Kawase Yoshimoto - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Kawase Tadayori - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Aramatsu Tadanari - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Kohara Hayashimoto - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Komon Yukikiyo - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Tsuchiya Munekiyo - Killed in Hachiman in 1342. == History == The origins of Nawa Shrine trace back to the Shōō and Meireki eras (1652-1658), when local people built a small shrine at the site of Nawa Nagatoshi's residence to honor his virtues. In Enpō 5 (1677), the [[Tottori Domain|Lord of Tottori]], [[Ikeda Mitsunaka]], revered Nagatoshi and built a new shrine at the Hiyoshizaka Sannō Gongen site, naming it Ujidono Gongen. According to the explanations of the early Meiji era, the revival of the shrine was initiated by Mitsunaka's son, [[Ikeda Tsunakiyo]], who allocated a small amount of festival funds each year. During the late Edo period, the domain lord [[Ikeda Yoshitake]] intended to appoint a priest for the shrine but was interrupted by the abolition of feudal domains and the establishment of prefectures. In 1873 (Meiji 7), the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine and renamed Ujidono Shrine. On June 22, 1876 (Meiji 9), Tottori Prefecture requested the Ministry of Home Affairs to elevate Ujidono Shrine to the rank of a national shrine, citing the accomplishments of the Nawa clan comparable to those of the Kusunoki clan. In October, the shrine priest Kusakaya Suee submitted a similar request to the Ministry of Education, and in November, Ikeda Yoshitake petitioned the Imperial Household Ministry for the shrine's elevation. On December 16, Shimane Prefecture, which had incorporated Tottori Prefecture at that time, supported these requests to the Ministry of Education. In the summer of 1877 (Meiji 10), Ikeda Yoshitake visited Ujidono Shrine during a journey from Shimane Prefecture to Tottori and witnessed its dilapidated state. Shortly afterward, Yoshitake passed away, and on November 28, his son, [[Ikeda Terutomo]], petitioned for the shrine's elevation, fulfilling his father's will. The Ministry of Home Affairs had already decided to rank the shrine as a special government shrine, and in response to Terutomo's petition, recommended the implementation to the Dajōkan (Great Council of State). Consequently, on January 10, 1878 (Meiji 11), the Dajōkan decided to rename Ujidono Shrine to Nawa Shrine and rank it as a special government shrine, also enshrining the spirits of Nawa Nagashige and other warriors. In the same year, on May 11, 1878, the Ministry of the Imperial Household decided to set the shrine's festival date to August 15, converting the date of Nagatoshi's death from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. On May 21, the Dajōkan officially established the festival date. Plans for reconstructing the dilapidated shrine buildings were devised by Shimane Prefecture and the Ministry of Home Affairs, with construction permits obtained on January 17, 1879 (Meiji 12), projecting government funding of 4769 yen, 21 sen, and 6 rin over three years. In 1881 (Meiji 14), further external structures and facilities were constructed at government expense. The shrine buildings were completed in 1882 (Meiji 15), and the relocation ceremony was held on March 10, 1883 (Meiji 16), with Tottori Prefectural Governor [[Yamada Nobumichi]] serving as the imperial envoy, presenting a new mirror from the Imperial Household Ministry as the shrine's sacred object. == Timeline == * [[June 22]], 1876 (Meiji 9): Tottori Prefecture requests the Ministry of Home Affairs to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a national shrine. * October 28: Kusakaya Suee requests the Ministry of Education to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a special government shrine. * November: Ikeda Yoshitake requests the Imperial Household Ministry to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a special government shrine. * December 16: Shimane Prefecture supports the requests to the Ministry of Education. * Summer 1877 (Meiji 10): Ikeda Yoshitake visits Ujidono Shrine. * October 20: The Ministry of Home Affairs decides to rank the shrine as a special government shrine. * November 28: Ikeda Terutomo petitions the Imperial Household Ministry to fulfill his father's will. * December 11: The Ministry of Home Affairs submits the petition to the Dajōkan. * [[January 10]], 1878 (Meiji 11): The Dajōkan decides to rename Ujidono Shrine to Nawa Shrine and rank it as a special government shrine. * May 11: The Ministry of the Imperial Household requests the Dajōkan to set the festival date to August 15. * May 21: The Dajōkan establishes the festival date as August 15. * June 19: Shimane Prefecture submits the shrine building reconstruction plan to the Ministry of Home Affairs. * August 31: The Ministry of Home Affairs issues instructions to Shimane Prefecture regarding the shrine building reconstruction plan. * November 15: Shimane Prefecture submits a revised plan with location maps. * December 21: The Ministry of Home Affairs submits a petition to the Dajōkan for the shrine building reconstruction. * December 25: The Dajōkan's investigation bureau recommends the approval of the reconstruction. * [[January 17]], 1879 (Meiji 12): The Dajōkan approves the reconstruction of Nawa Shrine. * October 30, 1879: The Dajōkan determines the order of Nawa Shrine among special government shrines, placing it after Minatogawa Shrine. * [[March 10]], 1883 (Meiji 16): The relocation ceremony. == References == {{Reflist}} === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="Annotations"}} === Sources === {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Yamada Yonekichi, ed., "History of Bekkaku Kanpeishas", pp. 24-27, Niroku Koshinjo, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * Okada Yoneo, "History of the Establishment of Shrines and Shrines", in Matsuyama Noho, ed., "One Hundred Years of Meiji Restoration Shinto History" Vol. 2, Shinto Culture Association, 1966. * "Daijōruiten", viewed in October 2011 at the [https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/ National Archives of Japan Digital Archive]. == Related Literature == *安津素彦・梅田義彦編集兼監修者『神道辞典』神社新報社、1968年、44頁 *白井永二・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、258-259頁 == See Also == {{commonscat|Nawa-jinja}} * [[Chōkō-ji Temple]] * [[Tsubota Shrine]] == External Links == * [http://www.daisen.jp/p/2/area/umigawa/30/ Daisen Town (Nawa Shrine)] {{DEFAULTSORT:Nawa Shrine}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Nawa Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Nawa Shrine]] [[Category:One Hundred Best Buildings of the Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Takamichi - Nephew of Nagashige, second son of Suketaka. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takamasa - Younger brother of Takamichi. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Nagauji - Nephew of Nagatoshi, third son of Yukiuji. Killed in Hachiman in 1340. * Nawa Sadauji - Younger brother of Nagauji. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takakami - Nephew of Nagatoshi, second son of Takashige. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takanaga - Nephew of Nagatoshi, second son of Takakane. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Yukishige - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Yukimura. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Hidemura - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Yorimura. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Gorōbyōe-no-jō - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Koremura. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Shigemura - Younger brother of Gorōbyōe-no-jō. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Okimura - Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Nobusada - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Yukisada. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Hiromasa - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nobusada, second son of Hiromasa. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Hirotsugu - Grandson of Nobusada's cousin. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Sukesada - Cousin of Nagatoshi, younger brother of Nobusada. Killed in Nijo Domiya in 1333. * Nawa Sukeshige - Cousin of Nagatoshi, younger brother of Sukesada. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Nagashige - Eldest son of Nagasada. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Takanobu - Eldest son of Takayuki. Killed in Tomioka, Bizen Province in 1343. * Nawa Yukizane - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Yukitada. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Sukekuni - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Takasuke. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takakuni - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin, eldest son of Sukekuni. Killed in Sakainan, Echizen Province in 1336. * Uchikawa Masanobu - Retainer of Nagatoshi. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Masachika - Second son of Uchikawa Masanobu. Committed suicide at Mt. Shosha in Harima Province in 1334. * Uchikawa Masakazu - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, eldest son of Nagaaki. Killed in Nijo Domiya in 1333. * Uchikawa Masayuki - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, younger brother of Masakazu. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Yuki - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, younger brother of Masayuki. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Yoshihiro - Grandson of Uchikawa Masanobu, fourth son of Yoshimasa. Killed in Higo Province in 1378. * Uchikawa Yukikage - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Uchikawa Takekage - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Uchikawa Kunitoki - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Kawase Yoshimoto - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Kawase Tadayori - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Aramatsu Tadanari - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Kohara Hayashimoto - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Komon Yukikiyo - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Tsuchiya Munekiyo - Killed in Hachiman in 1342. == History == The origins of Nawa Shrine trace back to the Shōō and Meireki eras (1652-1658), when local people built a small shrine at the site of Nawa Nagatoshi's residence to honor his virtues. In Enpō 5 (1677), the [[Tottori Domain|Lord of Tottori]], [[Ikeda Mitsunaka]], revered Nagatoshi and built a new shrine at the Hiyoshizaka Sannō Gongen site, naming it Ujidono Gongen. In October, the shrine priest Kusakaya Suee submitted a similar request to the Ministry of Education, and in November, Ikeda Yoshitake petitioned the Imperial Household Ministry for the shrine's elevation. On December 16, Shimane Prefecture, which had incorporated Tottori Prefecture at that time, supported these requests to the Ministry of Education. In the summer of 1877 (Meiji 10), Ikeda Yoshitake visited Ujidono Shrine during a journey from Shimane Prefecture to Tottori and witnessed its dilapidated state. Shortly afterward, Yoshitake passed away, and on November 28, his son, [[Ikeda Terutomo]], petitioned for the shrine's elevation, fulfilling his father's will. The Ministry of Home Affairs had already decided to rank the shrine as a special government shrine, and in response to Terutomo's petition, recommended the implementation to the Dajōkan (Great Council of State). Consequently, on January 10, 1878 (Meiji 11), the Dajōkan decided to rename Ujidono Shrine to Nawa Shrine and rank it as a special government shrine, also enshrining the spirits of Nawa Nagashige and other warriors. In the same year, on May 11, 1878, the Ministry of the Imperial Household decided to set the shrine's festival date to August 15, converting the date of Nagatoshi's death from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. On May 21, the Dajōkan officially established the festival date. Plans for reconstructing the dilapidated shrine buildings were devised by Shimane Prefecture and the Ministry of Home Affairs, with construction permits obtained on January 17, 1879 (Meiji 12), projecting government funding of 4769 yen, 21 sen, and 6 rin over three years. In 1881 (Meiji 14), further external structures and facilities were constructed at government expense. The shrine buildings were completed in 1882 (Meiji 15), and the relocation ceremony was held on March 10, 1883 (Meiji 16), with Tottori Prefectural Governor [[Yamada Nobumichi]] serving as the imperial envoy, presenting a new mirror from the Imperial Household Ministry as the shrine's sacred object. == Timeline == * [[June 22]], 1876 (Meiji 9): Tottori Prefecture requests the Ministry of Home Affairs to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a national shrine. * October 28: Kusakaya Suee requests the Ministry of Education to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a special government shrine. * November: Ikeda Yoshitake requests the Imperial Household Ministry to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a special government shrine. * December 16: Shimane Prefecture supports the requests to the Ministry of Education. * Summer 1877 (Meiji 10): Ikeda Yoshitake visits Ujidono Shrine. * October 20: The Ministry of Home Affairs decides to rank the shrine as a special government shrine. * November 28: Ikeda Terutomo petitions the Imperial Household Ministry to fulfill his father's will. * December 11: The Ministry of Home Affairs submits the petition to the Dajōkan. * [[January 10]], 1878 (Meiji 11): The Dajōkan decides to rename Ujidono Shrine to Nawa Shrine and rank it as a special government shrine. * May 11: The Ministry of the Imperial Household requests the Dajōkan to set the festival date to August 15. * May 21: The Dajōkan establishes the festival date as August 15. * June 19: Shimane Prefecture submits the shrine building reconstruction plan to the Ministry of Home Affairs. * August 31: The Ministry of Home Affairs issues instructions to Shimane Prefecture regarding the shrine building reconstruction plan. * November 15: Shimane Prefecture submits a revised plan with location maps. * December 21: The Ministry of Home Affairs submits a petition to the Dajōkan for the shrine building reconstruction. * December 25: The Dajōkan's investigation bureau recommends the approval of the reconstruction. * [[January 17]], 1879 (Meiji 12): The Dajōkan approves the reconstruction of Nawa Shrine. * October 30, 1879: The Dajōkan determines the order of Nawa Shrine among special government shrines, placing it after Minatogawa Shrine. * [[March 10]], 1883 (Meiji 16): The relocation ceremony. == References == {{Reflist}} === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="Annotations"}} === Sources === Collection)] * Okada Yoneo, "History of the Establishment of Shrines and Shrines", in Matsuyama Noho, ed., "One Hundred Years of Meiji Restoration Shinto History" Vol. 2, Shinto Culture Association, {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tottori Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Nawa Jinja'''|名和神社}} | image = Nawa-jinja Rōmon.jpg | caption = Nawa Jinja in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|30|16|N|133|29|42|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nawa Shrine | image = [[File:Nawa-jinja Worship Hall.jpg|260px]]
Worship Hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=260}} | location = 556 Nawa, Daisen-chō, Saihaku-gun, Tottori-ken | coordinates = {{Coord|35|30|16|N|133|29|42|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | deity = Nawa Nagatoshi | Shrine rank = Bekkaku Kanpeisha • Beppyo Jinja | Main Building Style = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = [[May 7]] }} {{nihongo|'''Nawa Jinja'''|名和神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is celebrated for its [[cherry blossoms]].{{cite web |url=http://yokoso.pref.tottori.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=1873 |title=Nawa Jinja |publisher=[[Tottori Prefecture]] |accessdate=23 April 2011 |url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723211712/http://yokoso.pref.tottori.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=1873 |archivedate=23 July 2011 }} It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]], dedicated to the memory of [[Nawa Nagatoshi]]. ==Overview== '''Nawa Shrine''' (名和神社, Nawa Jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen-chō]], [[Tottori Prefecture]]. The shrine enshrines [[Nawa Nagatoshi]]. Its former shrine ranking under the modern system was that of a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The shrine's crest (kamon) is the "Ho Kagari Bune" of the Nawa clan. [[File:Nawa-jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Nawa-jinja Rōmon.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Rōmon gate]] == Deities == Nawa Shrine enshrines Nawa Nagatoshi as the main deity, along with 42 members of the Nawa clan. Nawa Nagatoshi was a samurai who supported Emperor Go-Daigo in overthrowing the Kamakura shogunate and fought for the Southern Court during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. * [[Nawa Yoshitaka]] - Eldest son of Nagatoshi. Killed in the Battle of Sakaiura in 1338. * [[Nawa Takamitsu]] - Third son of Nagatoshi. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Nawa Yasunaga - Younger brother of Nagatoshi (third brother). Committed suicide in Izumo Province in 1333. * Nawa Yukitomo - Younger brother of Nagatoshi (ninth brother). Committed suicide in Senjōzan, Hōki Province in 1334. * Nawa Nagashige - Nephew of Nagatoshi. * Nawa Yoshishige - Nephew of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Nagayoshi. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Takamichi - Nephew of Nagashige, second son of Suketaka. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takamasa - Younger brother of Takamichi. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Nagauji - Nephew of Nagatoshi, third son of Yukiuji. Killed in Hachiman in 1340. * Nawa Sadauji - Younger brother of Nagauji. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takakami - Nephew of Nagatoshi, second son of Takashige. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takanaga - Nephew of Nagatoshi, second son of Takakane. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Yukishige - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Yukimura. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Hidemura - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Yorimura. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Gorōbyōe-no-jō - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Koremura. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Shigemura - Younger brother of Gorōbyōe-no-jō. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Okimura - Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Nobusada - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Yukisada. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Hiromasa - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nobusada, second son of Hiromasa. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Hirotsugu - Grandson of Nobusada's cousin. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Sukesada - Cousin of Nagatoshi, younger brother of Nobusada. Killed in Nijo Domiya in 1333. * Nawa Sukeshige - Cousin of Nagatoshi, younger brother of Sukesada. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Nagashige - Eldest son of Nagasada. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Takanobu - Eldest son of Takayuki. Killed in Tomioka, Bizen Province in 1343. * Nawa Yukizane - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Yukitada. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Sukekuni - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Takasuke. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takakuni - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin, eldest son of Sukekuni. Killed in Sakainan, Echizen Province in 1336. * Uchikawa Masanobu - Retainer of Nagatoshi. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Masachika - Second son of Uchikawa Masanobu. Committed suicide at Mt. Shosha in Harima Province in 1334. * Uchikawa Masakazu - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, eldest son of Nagaaki. Killed in Nijo Domiya in 1333. * Uchikawa Masayuki - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, younger brother of Masakazu. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Yuki - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, younger brother of Masayuki. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Yoshihiro - Grandson of Uchikawa Masanobu, fourth son of Yoshimasa. Killed in Higo Province in 1378. * Uchikawa Yukikage - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Uchikawa Takekage - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Uchikawa Kunitoki - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Kawase Yoshimoto - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Kawase Tadayori - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Aramatsu Tadanari - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Kohara Hayashimoto - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Komon Yukikiyo - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Tsuchiya Munekiyo - Killed in Hachiman in 1342. == History == The origins of Nawa Shrine trace back to the Shōō and Meireki eras (1652-1658), when local people built a small shrine at the site of Nawa Nagatoshi's residence to honor his virtues. In Enpō 5 (1677), the [[Tottori Domain|Lord of Tottori]], [[Ikeda Mitsunaka]], revered Nagatoshi and built a new shrine at the Hiyoshizaka Sannō Gongen site, naming it Ujidono Gongen. According to the explanations of the early Meiji era, the revival of the shrine was initiated by Mitsunaka's son, [[Ikeda Tsunakiyo]], who allocated a small amount of festival funds each year. During the late Edo period, the domain lord [[Ikeda Yoshitake]] intended to appoint a priest for the shrine but was interrupted by the abolition of feudal domains and the establishment of prefectures. In 1873 (Meiji 7), the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine and renamed Ujidono Shrine. On June 22, 1876 (Meiji 9), Tottori Prefecture requested the Ministry of Home Affairs to elevate Ujidono Shrine to the rank of a national shrine, citing the accomplishments of the Nawa clan comparable to those of the Kusunoki clan. In October, the shrine priest Kusakaya Suee submitted a similar request to the Ministry of Education, and in November, Ikeda Yoshitake petitioned the Imperial Household Ministry for the shrine's elevation. On December 16, Shimane Prefecture, which had incorporated Tottori Prefecture at that time, supported these requests to the Ministry of Education. In the summer of 1877 (Meiji 10), Ikeda Yoshitake visited Ujidono Shrine during a journey from Shimane Prefecture to Tottori and witnessed its dilapidated state. Shortly afterward, Yoshitake passed away, and on November 28, his son, [[Ikeda Terutomo]], petitioned for the shrine's elevation, fulfilling his father's will. The Ministry of Home Affairs had already decided to rank the shrine as a special government shrine, and in response to Terutomo's petition, recommended the implementation to the Dajōkan (Great Council of State). Consequently, on January 10, 1878 (Meiji 11), the Dajōkan decided to rename Ujidono Shrine to Nawa Shrine and rank it as a special government shrine, also enshrining the spirits of Nawa Nagashige and other warriors. In the same year, on May 11, 1878, the Ministry of the Imperial Household decided to set the shrine's festival date to August 15, converting the date of Nagatoshi's death from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. On May 21, the Dajōkan officially established the festival date. Plans for reconstructing the dilapidated shrine buildings were devised by Shimane Prefecture and the Ministry of Home Affairs, with construction permits obtained on January 17, 1879 (Meiji 12), projecting government funding of 4769 yen, 21 sen, and 6 rin over three years. In 1881 (Meiji 14), further external structures and facilities were constructed at government expense. The shrine buildings were completed in 1882 (Meiji 15), and the relocation ceremony was held on March 10, 1883 (Meiji 16), with Tottori Prefectural Governor [[Yamada Nobumichi]] serving as the imperial envoy, presenting a new mirror from the Imperial Household Ministry as the shrine's sacred object. == Timeline == * [[June 22]], 1876 (Meiji 9): Tottori Prefecture requests the Ministry of Home Affairs to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a national shrine. * October 28: Kusakaya Suee requests the Ministry of Education to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a special government shrine. * November: Ikeda Yoshitake requests the Imperial Household Ministry to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a special government shrine. * December 16: Shimane Prefecture supports the requests to the Ministry of Education. * Summer 1877 (Meiji 10): Ikeda Yoshitake visits Ujidono Shrine. * October 20: The Ministry of Home Affairs decides to rank the shrine as a special government shrine. * November 28: Ikeda Terutomo petitions the Imperial Household Ministry to fulfill his father's will. * December 11: The Ministry of Home Affairs submits the petition to the Dajōkan. * [[January 10]], 1878 (Meiji 11): The Dajōkan decides to rename Ujidono Shrine to Nawa Shrine and rank it as a special government shrine. * May 11: The Ministry of the Imperial Household requests the Dajōkan to set the festival date to August 15. * May 21: The Dajōkan establishes the festival date as August 15. * June 19: Shimane Prefecture submits the shrine building reconstruction plan to the Ministry of Home Affairs. * August 31: The Ministry of Home Affairs issues instructions to Shimane Prefecture regarding the shrine building reconstruction plan. * November 15: Shimane Prefecture submits a revised plan with location maps. * December 21: The Ministry of Home Affairs submits a petition to the Dajōkan for the shrine building reconstruction. * December 25: The Dajōkan's investigation bureau recommends the approval of the reconstruction. * [[January 17]], 1879 (Meiji 12): The Dajōkan approves the reconstruction of Nawa Shrine. * October 30, 1879: The Dajōkan determines the order of Nawa Shrine among special government shrines, placing it after Minatogawa Shrine. * [[March 10]], 1883 (Meiji 16): The relocation ceremony. == References == {{Reflist}} === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="Annotations"}} === Sources === Collection)] * Okada Yoneo, "History of the Establishment of Shrines and Shrines", in Matsuyama Noho, ed., "One Hundred Years of Meiji Restoration Shinto History" Vol. 2, Shinto Culture Association, 1966. * "Daijōruiten", viewed in October 2011 at the [https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/ National Archives of Japan Digital Archive]. == Related Literature == *安津素彦・梅田義彦編集兼監修者『神道辞典』神社新報社、1968年、44頁 *白井永二・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、258-259頁 == See Also == {{commonscat|Nawa-jinja}} * [[Chōkō-ji Temple]] * [[Tsubota Shrine]] == External Links == * [http://www.daisen.jp/p/2/area/umigawa/30/ Daisen Town (Nawa Shrine)] {{DEFAULTSORT:Nawa Shrine}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Nawa Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages Worship Hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=260}} | location = 556 Nawa, Daisen-chō, Saihaku-gun, Tottori-ken | coordinates = {{Coord|35|30|16|N|133|29|42|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | deity = Nawa Nagatoshi | Shrine rank = Bekkaku Kanpeisha • Beppyo Jinja | Main Building Style = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = [[May 7]] }} {{nihongo|'''Nawa Jinja'''|名和神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is celebrated for its [[cherry blossoms]].{{cite web |url=http://yokoso.pref.tottori.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=1873 |title=Nawa Jinja |publisher=[[Tottori Prefecture]] |accessdate=23 April 2011 |url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723211712/http://yokoso.pref.tottori.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=1873 |archivedate=23 July 2011 }} It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]], dedicated to the memory of [[Nawa Nagatoshi]]. ==Overview== '''Nawa Shrine''' (名和神社, Nawa Jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen-chō]], [[Tottori Prefecture]]. The shrine enshrines [[Nawa Nagatoshi]]. Its former shrine ranking under the modern system was that of a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The shrine's crest (kamon) is the "Ho Kagari Bune" of the Nawa clan. [[File:Nawa-jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Nawa-jinja Rōmon.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Rōmon gate]] == Deities == Nawa Shrine enshrines Nawa Nagatoshi as the main deity, along with 42 members of the Nawa clan. Nawa Nagatoshi was a samurai who supported Emperor Go-Daigo in overthrowing the Kamakura shogunate and fought for the Southern Court during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. * [[Nawa Yoshitaka]] - Eldest son of Nagatoshi. Killed in the Battle of Sakaiura in 1338. * [[Nawa Takamitsu]] - Third son of Nagatoshi. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Nawa Yasunaga - Younger brother of Nagatoshi (third brother). Committed suicide in Izumo Province in 1333. * Nawa Yukitomo - Younger brother of Nagatoshi (ninth brother). Committed suicide in Senjōzan, Hōki Province in 1334. * Nawa Nagashige - Nephew of Nagatoshi. * Nawa Yoshishige - Nephew of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Nagayoshi. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Takamichi - Nephew of Nagashige, second son of Suketaka. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takamasa - Younger brother of Takamichi. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Nagauji - Nephew of Nagatoshi, third son of Yukiuji. Killed in Hachiman in 1340. * Nawa Sadauji - Younger brother of Nagauji. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takakami - Nephew of Nagatoshi, second son of Takashige. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takanaga - Nephew of Nagatoshi, second son of Takakane. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Yukishige - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Yukimura. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Hidemura - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Yorimura. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Gorōbyōe-no-jō - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Koremura. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Shigemura - Younger brother of Gorōbyōe-no-jō. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Okimura - Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Nobusada - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Yukisada. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Hiromasa - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nobusada, second son of Hiromasa. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Hirotsugu - Grandson of Nobusada's cousin. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Sukesada - Cousin of Nagatoshi, younger brother of Nobusada. Killed in Nijo Domiya in 1333. * Nawa Sukeshige - Cousin of Nagatoshi, younger brother of Sukesada. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Nagashige - Eldest son of Nagasada. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Takanobu - Eldest son of Takayuki. Killed in Tomioka, Bizen Province in 1343. * Nawa Yukizane - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Yukitada. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Sukekuni - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Takasuke. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takakuni - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin, eldest son of Sukekuni. Killed in Sakainan, Echizen Province in 1336. * Uchikawa Masanobu - Retainer of Nagatoshi. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Masachika - Second son of Uchikawa Masanobu. Committed suicide at Mt. Shosha in Harima Province in 1334. * Uchikawa Masakazu - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, eldest son of Nagaaki. Killed in Nijo Domiya in 1333. * Uchikawa Masayuki - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, younger brother of Masakazu. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Yuki - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, younger brother of Masayuki. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Yoshihiro - Grandson of Uchikawa Masanobu, fourth son of Yoshimasa. Killed in Higo Province in 1378. * Uchikawa Yukikage - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Uchikawa Takekage - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Uchikawa Kunitoki - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Kawase Yoshimoto - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Kawase Tadayori - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Aramatsu Tadanari - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Kohara Hayashimoto - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Komon Yukikiyo - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Tsuchiya Munekiyo - Killed in Hachiman in 1342. == History == The origins of Nawa Shrine trace back to the Shōō and Meireki eras (1652-1658), when local people built a small shrine at the site of Nawa Nagatoshi's residence to honor his virtues. In Enpō 5 (1677), the [[Tottori Domain|Lord of Tottori]], [[Ikeda Mitsunaka]], revered Nagatoshi and built a new shrine at the Hiyoshizaka Sannō Gongen site, naming it Ujidono Gongen. According to the explanations of the early Meiji era, the revival of the shrine was initiated by Mitsunaka's son, [[Ikeda Tsunakiyo]], who allocated a small amount of festival funds each year. During the late Edo period, the domain lord [[Ikeda Yoshitake]] intended to appoint a priest for the shrine but was interrupted by the abolition of feudal domains and the establishment of prefectures. In 1873 (Meiji 7), the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine and renamed Ujidono Shrine. On June 22, 1876 (Meiji 9), Tottori Prefecture requested the Ministry of Home Affairs to elevate Ujidono Shrine to the rank of a national shrine, citing the accomplishments of the Nawa clan comparable to those of the Kusunoki clan. In October, the shrine priest Kusakaya Suee submitted a similar request to the Ministry of Education, and in November, Ikeda Yoshitake petitioned the Imperial Household Ministry for the shrine's elevation. On December 16, Shimane Prefecture, which had incorporated Tottori Prefecture at that time, supported these requests to the Ministry of Education. In the summer of 1877 (Meiji 10), Ikeda Yoshitake visited Ujidono Shrine during a journey from Shimane Prefecture to Tottori and witnessed its dilapidated state. Shortly afterward, Yoshitake passed away, and on November 28, his son, [[Ikeda Terutomo]], petitioned for the shrine's elevation, fulfilling his father's will. The Ministry of Home Affairs had already decided to rank the shrine as a special government shrine, and in response to Terutomo's petition, recommended the implementation to the Dajōkan (Great Council of State). Consequently, on January 10, 1878 (Meiji 11), the Dajōkan decided to rename Ujidono Shrine to Nawa Shrine and rank it as a special government shrine, also enshrining the spirits of Nawa Nagashige and other warriors. In the same year, on May 11, 1878, the Ministry of the Imperial Household decided to set the shrine's festival date to August 15, converting the date of Nagatoshi's death from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. On May 21, the Dajōkan officially established the festival date. Plans for reconstructing the dilapidated shrine buildings were devised by Shimane Prefecture and the Ministry of Home Affairs, with construction permits obtained on January 17, 1879 (Meiji 12), projecting government funding of 4769 yen, 21 sen, and 6 rin over three years. In 1881 (Meiji 14), further external structures and facilities were constructed at government expense. The shrine buildings were completed in 1882 (Meiji 15), and the relocation ceremony was held on March 10, 1883 (Meiji 16), with Tottori Prefectural Governor [[Yamada Nobumichi]] serving as the imperial envoy, presenting a new mirror from the Imperial Household Ministry as the shrine's sacred object. == Timeline == * [[June 22]], 1876 (Meiji 9): Tottori Prefecture requests the Ministry of Home Affairs to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a national shrine. * October 28: Kusakaya Suee requests the Ministry of Education to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a special government shrine. * November: Ikeda Yoshitake requests the Imperial Household Ministry to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a special government shrine. * December 16: Shimane Prefecture supports the requests to the Ministry of Education. * Summer 1877 (Meiji 10): Ikeda Yoshitake visits Ujidono Shrine. * October 20: The Ministry of Home Affairs decides to rank the shrine as a special government shrine. * November 28: Ikeda Terutomo petitions the Imperial Household Ministry to fulfill his father's will. * December 11: The Ministry of Home Affairs submits the petition to the Dajōkan. * [[January 10]], 1878 (Meiji 11): The Dajōkan decides to rename Ujidono Shrine to Nawa Shrine and rank it as a special government shrine. * May 11: The Ministry of the Imperial Household requests the Dajōkan to set the festival date to August 15. * May 21: The Dajōkan establishes the festival date as August 15. * June 19: Shimane Prefecture submits the shrine building reconstruction plan to the Ministry of Home Affairs. * August 31: The Ministry of Home Affairs issues instructions to Shimane Prefecture regarding the shrine building reconstruction plan. * November 15: Shimane Prefecture submits a revised plan with location maps. * December 21: The Ministry of Home Affairs submits a petition to the Dajōkan for the shrine building reconstruction. * December 25: The Dajōkan's investigation bureau recommends the approval of the reconstruction. * [[January 17]], 1879 (Meiji 12): The Dajōkan approves the reconstruction of Nawa Shrine. * October 30, 1879: The Dajōkan determines the order of Nawa Shrine among special government shrines, placing it after Minatogawa Shrine. * [[March 10]], 1883 (Meiji 16): The relocation ceremony. == References == {{Reflist}} === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="Annotations"}} === Sources === Collection)] * Okada Yoneo, "History of the Establishment of Shrines and Shrines", in Matsuyama Noho, ed., "One Hundred Years of Meiji Restoration Shinto History" Vol. 2, Shinto Culture Association, {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tottori Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Nawa Jinja'''|名和神社}} | image = Nawa-jinja Rōmon.jpg | caption = Nawa Jinja in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|30|16|N|133|29|42|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nawa Shrine | image = [[File:Nawa-jinja Worship Hall.jpg|260px]]
Worship Hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=260}} | location = 556 Nawa, Daisen-chō, Saihaku-gun, Tottori-ken | coordinates = {{Coord|35|30|16|N|133|29|42|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | deity = Nawa Nagatoshi | Shrine rank = Bekkaku Kanpeisha • Beppyo Jinja | Main Building Style = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = [[May 7]] }} {{nihongo|'''Nawa Jinja'''|名和神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is celebrated for its [[cherry blossoms]].{{cite web |url=http://yokoso.pref.tottori.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=1873 |title=Nawa Jinja |publisher=[[Tottori Prefecture]] |accessdate=23 April 2011 |url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723211712/http://yokoso.pref.tottori.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=1873 |archivedate=23 July 2011 }} It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]], dedicated to the memory of [[Nawa Nagatoshi]]. ==Overview== '''Nawa Shrine''' (名和神社, Nawa Jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen-chō]], [[Tottori Prefecture]]. The shrine enshrines [[Nawa Nagatoshi]]. Its former shrine ranking under the modern system was that of a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The shrine's crest (kamon) is the "Ho Kagari Bune" of the Nawa clan. [[File:Nawa-jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Nawa-jinja Rōmon.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Rōmon gate]] == Deities == Nawa Shrine enshrines Nawa Nagatoshi as the main deity, along with 42 members of the Nawa clan. Nawa Nagatoshi was a samurai who supported Emperor Go-Daigo in overthrowing the Kamakura shogunate and fought for the Southern Court during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. * [[Nawa Yoshitaka]] - Eldest son of Nagatoshi. Killed in the Battle of Sakaiura in 1338. * [[Nawa Takamitsu]] - Third son of Nagatoshi. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Nawa Yasunaga - Younger brother of Nagatoshi (third brother). Committed suicide in Izumo Province in 1333. * Nawa Yukitomo - Younger brother of Nagatoshi (ninth brother). Committed suicide in Senjōzan, Hōki Province in 1334. * Nawa Nagashige - Nephew of Nagatoshi. * Nawa Yoshishige - Nephew of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Nagayoshi. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Takamichi - Nephew of Nagashige, second son of Suketaka. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takamasa - Younger brother of Takamichi. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Nagauji - Nephew of Nagatoshi, third son of Yukiuji. Killed in Hachiman in 1340. * Nawa Sadauji - Younger brother of Nagauji. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takakami - Nephew of Nagatoshi, second son of Takashige. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takanaga - Nephew of Nagatoshi, second son of Takakane. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Yukishige - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Yukimura. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Hidemura - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Yorimura. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Gorōbyōe-no-jō - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Koremura. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Shigemura - Younger brother of Gorōbyōe-no-jō. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Okimura - Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Nobusada - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Yukisada. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Hiromasa - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nobusada, second son of Hiromasa. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Hirotsugu - Grandson of Nobusada's cousin. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Sukesada - Cousin of Nagatoshi, younger brother of Nobusada. Killed in Nijo Domiya in 1333. * Nawa Sukeshige - Cousin of Nagatoshi, younger brother of Sukesada. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Nagashige - Eldest son of Nagasada. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Takanobu - Eldest son of Takayuki. Killed in Tomioka, Bizen Province in 1343. * Nawa Yukizane - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Yukitada. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Sukekuni - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Takasuke. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takakuni - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin, eldest son of Sukekuni. Killed in Sakainan, Echizen Province in 1336. * Uchikawa Masanobu - Retainer of Nagatoshi. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Masachika - Second son of Uchikawa Masanobu. Committed suicide at Mt. Shosha in Harima Province in 1334. * Uchikawa Masakazu - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, eldest son of Nagaaki. Killed in Nijo Domiya in 1333. * Uchikawa Masayuki - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, younger brother of Masakazu. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Yuki - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, younger brother of Masayuki. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Yoshihiro - Grandson of Uchikawa Masanobu, fourth son of Yoshimasa. Killed in Higo Province in 1378. * Uchikawa Yukikage - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Uchikawa Takekage - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Uchikawa Kunitoki - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Kawase Yoshimoto - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Kawase Tadayori - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Aramatsu Tadanari - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Kohara Hayashimoto - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Komon Yukikiyo - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Tsuchiya Munekiyo - Killed in Hachiman in 1342. == History == The origins of Nawa Shrine trace back to the Shōō and Meireki eras (1652-1658), when local people built a small shrine at the site of Nawa Nagatoshi's residence to honor his virtues. In Enpō 5 (1677), the [[Tottori Domain|Lord of Tottori]], [[Ikeda Mitsunaka]], revered Nagatoshi and built a new shrine at the Hiyoshizaka Sannō Gongen site, naming it Ujidono Gongen. According to the explanations of the early Meiji era, the revival of the shrine was initiated by Mitsunaka's son, [[Ikeda Tsunakiyo]], who allocated a small amount of festival funds each year. During the late Edo period, the domain lord [[Ikeda Yoshitake]] intended to appoint a priest for the shrine but was interrupted by the abolition of feudal domains and the establishment of prefectures. In 1873 (Meiji 7), the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine and renamed Ujidono Shrine. On June 22, 1876 (Meiji 9), Tottori Prefecture requested the Ministry of Home Affairs to elevate Ujidono Shrine to the rank of a national shrine, citing the accomplishments of the Nawa clan comparable to those of the Kusunoki clan. In October, the shrine priest Kusakaya Suee submitted a similar request to the Ministry of Education, and in November, Ikeda Yoshitake petitioned the Imperial Household Ministry for the shrine's elevation. On December 16, Shimane Prefecture, which had incorporated Tottori Prefecture at that time, supported these requests to the Ministry of Education. In the summer of 1877 (Meiji 10), Ikeda Yoshitake visited Ujidono Shrine during a journey from Shimane Prefecture to Tottori and witnessed its dilapidated state. Shortly afterward, Yoshitake passed away, and on November 28, his son, [[Ikeda Terutomo]], petitioned for the shrine's elevation, fulfilling his father's will. The Ministry of Home Affairs had already decided to rank the shrine as a special government shrine, and in response to Terutomo's petition, recommended the implementation to the Dajōkan (Great Council of State). Consequently, on January 10, 1878 (Meiji 11), the Dajōkan decided to rename Ujidono Shrine to Nawa Shrine and rank it as a special government shrine, also enshrining the spirits of Nawa Nagashige and other warriors. In the same year, on May 11, 1878, the Ministry of the Imperial Household decided to set the shrine's festival date to August 15, converting the date of Nagatoshi's death from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. On May 21, the Dajōkan officially established the festival date. Plans for reconstructing the dilapidated shrine buildings were devised by Shimane Prefecture and the Ministry of Home Affairs, with construction permits obtained on January 17, 1879 (Meiji 12), projecting government funding of 4769 yen, 21 sen, and 6 rin over three years. In 1881 (Meiji 14), further external structures and facilities were constructed at government expense. The shrine buildings were completed in 1882 (Meiji 15), and the relocation ceremony was held on March 10, 1883 (Meiji 16), with Tottori Prefectural Governor [[Yamada Nobumichi]] serving as the imperial envoy, presenting a new mirror from the Imperial Household Ministry as the shrine's sacred object. == Timeline == * [[June 22]], 1876 (Meiji 9): Tottori Prefecture requests the Ministry of Home Affairs to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a national shrine. * October 28: Kusakaya Suee requests the Ministry of Education to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a special government shrine. * November: Ikeda Yoshitake requests the Imperial Household Ministry to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a special government shrine. * December 16: Shimane Prefecture supports the requests to the Ministry of Education. * Summer 1877 (Meiji 10): Ikeda Yoshitake visits Ujidono Shrine. * October 20: The Ministry of Home Affairs decides to rank the shrine as a special government shrine. * November 28: Ikeda Terutomo petitions the Imperial Household Ministry to fulfill his father's will. * December 11: The Ministry of Home Affairs submits the petition to the Dajōkan. * [[January 10]], 1878 (Meiji 11): The Dajōkan decides to rename Ujidono Shrine to Nawa Shrine and rank it as a special government shrine. * May 11: The Ministry of the Imperial Household requests the Dajōkan to set the festival date to August 15. * May 21: The Dajōkan establishes the festival date as August 15. * June 19: Shimane Prefecture submits the shrine building reconstruction plan to the Ministry of Home Affairs. * August 31: The Ministry of Home Affairs issues instructions to Shimane Prefecture regarding the shrine building reconstruction plan. * November 15: Shimane Prefecture submits a revised plan with location maps. * December 21: The Ministry of Home Affairs submits a petition to the Dajōkan for the shrine building reconstruction. * December 25: The Dajōkan's investigation bureau recommends the approval of the reconstruction. * [[January 17]], 1879 (Meiji 12): The Dajōkan approves the reconstruction of Nawa Shrine. * October 30, 1879: The Dajōkan determines the order of Nawa Shrine among special government shrines, placing it after Minatogawa Shrine. * [[March 10]], 1883 (Meiji 16): The relocation ceremony. == References == {{Reflist}} === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="Annotations"}} === Sources === {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tottori Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Nawa Jinja'''|名和神社}} | image = Nawa-jinja Rōmon.jpg | caption = Nawa Jinja in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|30|16|N|133|29|42|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nawa Shrine | image = [[File:Nawa-jinja Worship Hall.jpg|260px]]
Worship Hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=260}} | location = 556 Nawa, Daisen-chō, Saihaku-gun, Tottori-ken | coordinates = {{Coord|35|30|16|N|133|29|42|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | deity = Nawa Nagatoshi | Shrine rank = Bekkaku Kanpeisha • Beppyo Jinja | Main Building Style = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = [[May 7]] }} {{nihongo|'''Nawa Jinja'''|名和神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is celebrated for its [[cherry blossoms]].{{cite web |url=http://yokoso.pref.tottori.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=1873 |title=Nawa Jinja |publisher=[[Tottori Prefecture]] |accessdate=23 April 2011 |url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723211712/http://yokoso.pref.tottori.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=1873 |archivedate=23 July 2011 }} It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]], dedicated to the memory of [[Nawa Nagatoshi]]. ==Overview== '''Nawa Shrine''' (名和神社, Nawa Jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen-chō]], [[Tottori Prefecture]]. The shrine enshrines [[Nawa Nagatoshi]]. Its former shrine ranking under the modern system was that of a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The shrine's crest (kamon) is the "Ho Kagari Bune" of the Nawa clan. [[File:Nawa-jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Nawa-jinja Rōmon.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Rōmon gate]] == Deities == Nawa Shrine enshrines Nawa Nagatoshi as the main deity, along with 42 members of the Nawa clan. Nawa Nagatoshi was a samurai who supported Emperor Go-Daigo in overthrowing the Kamakura shogunate and fought for the Southern Court during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. * [[Nawa Yoshitaka]] - Eldest son of Nagatoshi. Killed in the Battle of Sakaiura in 1338. * [[Nawa Takamitsu]] - Third son of Nagatoshi. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Nawa Yasunaga - Younger brother of Nagatoshi (third brother). Committed suicide in Izumo Province in 1333. * Nawa Yukitomo - Younger brother of Nagatoshi (ninth brother). Committed suicide in Senjōzan, Hōki Province in 1334. * Nawa Nagashige - Nephew of Nagatoshi. * Nawa Yoshishige - Nephew of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Nagayoshi. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Takamichi - Nephew of Nagashige, second son of Suketaka. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takamasa - Younger brother of Takamichi. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Nagauji - Nephew of Nagatoshi, third son of Yukiuji. Killed in Hachiman in 1340. * Nawa Sadauji - Younger brother of Nagauji. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takakami - Nephew of Nagatoshi, second son of Takashige. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takanaga - Nephew of Nagatoshi, second son of Takakane. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Yukishige - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Yukimura. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Hidemura - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Yorimura. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Gorōbyōe-no-jō - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Koremura. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Shigemura - Younger brother of Gorōbyōe-no-jō. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Okimura - Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Nobusada - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Yukisada. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Hiromasa - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nobusada, second son of Hiromasa. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Hirotsugu - Grandson of Nobusada's cousin. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Sukesada - Cousin of Nagatoshi, younger brother of Nobusada. Killed in Nijo Domiya in 1333. * Nawa Sukeshige - Cousin of Nagatoshi, younger brother of Sukesada. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Nagashige - Eldest son of Nagasada. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Takanobu - Eldest son of Takayuki. Killed in Tomioka, Bizen Province in 1343. * Nawa Yukizane - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Yukitada. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Sukekuni - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Takasuke. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takakuni - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin, eldest son of Sukekuni. Killed in Sakainan, Echizen Province in 1336. * Uchikawa Masanobu - Retainer of Nagatoshi. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Masachika - Second son of Uchikawa Masanobu. Committed suicide at Mt. Shosha in Harima Province in 1334. * Uchikawa Masakazu - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, eldest son of Nagaaki. Killed in Nijo Domiya in 1333. * Uchikawa Masayuki - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, younger brother of Masakazu. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Yuki - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, younger brother of Masayuki. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Yoshihiro - Grandson of Uchikawa Masanobu, fourth son of Yoshimasa. Killed in Higo Province in 1378. * Uchikawa Yukikage - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Uchikawa Takekage - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Uchikawa Kunitoki - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Kawase Yoshimoto - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Kawase Tadayori - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Aramatsu Tadanari - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Kohara Hayashimoto - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Komon Yukikiyo - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Tsuchiya Munekiyo - Killed in Hachiman in 1342. == History == The origins of Nawa Shrine trace back to the Shōō and Meireki eras (1652-1658), when local people built a small shrine at the site of Nawa Nagatoshi's residence to honor his virtues. In Enpō 5 (1677), the [[Tottori Domain|Lord of Tottori]], [[Ikeda Mitsunaka]], revered Nagatoshi and built a new shrine at the Hiyoshizaka Sannō Gongen site, naming it Ujidono Gongen. According to the explanations of the early Meiji era, the revival of the shrine was initiated by Mitsunaka's son, [[Ikeda Tsunakiyo]], who allocated a small amount of festival funds each year. During the late Edo period, the domain lord [[Ikeda Yoshitake]] intended to appoint a priest for the shrine but was interrupted by the abolition of feudal domains and the establishment of prefectures. In 1873 (Meiji 7), the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine and renamed Ujidono Shrine. On June 22, 1876 (Meiji 9), Tottori Prefecture requested the Ministry of Home Affairs to elevate Ujidono Shrine to the rank of a national shrine, citing the accomplishments of the Nawa clan comparable to those of the Kusunoki clan. In October, the shrine priest Kusakaya Suee submitted a similar request to the Ministry of Education, and in November, Ikeda Yoshitake petitioned the Imperial Household Ministry for the shrine's elevation. On December 16, Shimane Prefecture, which had incorporated Tottori Prefecture at that time, supported these requests to the Ministry of Education. In the summer of 1877 (Meiji 10), Ikeda Yoshitake visited Ujidono Shrine during a journey from Shimane Prefecture to Tottori and witnessed its dilapidated state. Shortly afterward, Yoshitake passed away, and on November 28, his son, [[Ikeda Terutomo]], petitioned for the shrine's elevation, fulfilling his father's will. The Ministry of Home Affairs had already decided to rank the shrine as a special government shrine, and in response to Terutomo's petition, recommended the implementation to the Dajōkan (Great Council of State). Consequently, on January 10, 1878 (Meiji 11), the Dajōkan decided to rename Ujidono Shrine to Nawa Shrine and rank it as a special government shrine, also enshrining the spirits of Nawa Nagashige and other warriors. In the same year, on May 11, 1878, the Ministry of the Imperial Household decided to set the shrine's festival date to August 15, converting the date of Nagatoshi's death from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. On May 21, the Dajōkan officially established the festival date. Plans for reconstructing the dilapidated shrine buildings were devised by Shimane Prefecture and the Ministry of Home Affairs, with construction permits obtained on January 17, 1879 (Meiji 12), projecting government funding of 4769 yen, 21 sen, and 6 rin over three years. In 1881 (Meiji 14), further external structures and facilities were constructed at government expense. The shrine buildings were completed in 1882 (Meiji 15), and the relocation ceremony was held on March 10, 1883 (Meiji 16), with Tottori Prefectural Governor [[Yamada Nobumichi]] serving as the imperial envoy, presenting a new mirror from the Imperial Household Ministry as the shrine's sacred object. == Timeline == * [[June 22]], 1876 (Meiji 9): Tottori Prefecture requests the Ministry of Home Affairs to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a national shrine. * October 28: Kusakaya Suee requests the Ministry of Education to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a special government shrine. * November: Ikeda Yoshitake requests the Imperial Household Ministry to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a special government shrine. * December 16: Shimane Prefecture supports the requests to the Ministry of Education. * Summer 1877 (Meiji 10): Ikeda Yoshitake visits Ujidono Shrine. * October 20: The Ministry of Home Affairs decides to rank the shrine as a special government shrine. * November 28: Ikeda Terutomo petitions the Imperial Household Ministry to fulfill his father's will. * December 11: The Ministry of Home Affairs submits the petition to the Dajōkan. * [[January 10]], 1878 (Meiji 11): The Dajōkan decides to rename Ujidono Shrine to Nawa Shrine and rank it as a special government shrine. * May 11: The Ministry of the Imperial Household requests the Dajōkan to set the festival date to August 15. * May 21: The Dajōkan establishes the festival date as August 15. * June 19: Shimane Prefecture submits the shrine building reconstruction plan to the Ministry of Home Affairs. * August 31: The Ministry of Home Affairs issues instructions to Shimane Prefecture regarding the shrine building reconstruction plan. * November 15: Shimane Prefecture submits a revised plan with location maps. * December 21: The Ministry of Home Affairs submits a petition to the Dajōkan for the shrine building reconstruction. * December 25: The Dajōkan's investigation bureau recommends the approval of the reconstruction. * [[January 17]], 1879 (Meiji 12): The Dajōkan approves the reconstruction of Nawa Shrine. * October 30, 1879: The Dajōkan determines the order of Nawa Shrine among special government shrines, placing it after Minatogawa Shrine. * [[March 10]], 1883 (Meiji 16): The relocation ceremony. == References == {{Reflist}} === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="Annotations"}} === Sources === {{Reflist|30em}} == Bibliography == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Yamada Yonekichi, ed., "History of Bekkaku Kanpeishas", pp. 24-27, Niroku Koshinjo, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * Okada Yoneo, "History of the Establishment of Shrines and Shrines", in Matsuyama Noho, ed., "One Hundred Years of Meiji Restoration Shinto History" Vol. 2, Shinto Culture Association, 1966. * "Daijōruiten", viewed in October 2011 at the [https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/ National Archives of Japan Digital Archive]. == Related Literature == *安津素彦・梅田義彦編集兼監修者『神道辞典』神社新報社、1968年、44頁 *白井永二・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、258-259頁 == See Also == {{commonscat|Nawa-jinja}} * [[Chōkō-ji Temple]] * [[Tsubota Shrine]] == External Links == * [http://www.daisen.jp/p/2/area/umigawa/30/ Daisen Town (Nawa Shrine)] {{DEFAULTSORT:Nawa Shrine}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Nawa Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages It is celebrated for its [[cherry blossoms]].{{cite web |url=http://yokoso.pref.tottori.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=1873 |title=Nawa Jinja |publisher=[[Tottori Prefecture]] |accessdate=23 April 2011 |url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723211712/http://yokoso.pref.tottori.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=1873 |archivedate=23 July 2011 }} It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]], dedicated to the memory of [[Nawa Nagatoshi]]. ==Overview== '''Nawa Shrine''' (名和神社, Nawa Jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen-chō]], [[Tottori Prefecture]]. The shrine enshrines [[Nawa Nagatoshi]]. Its former shrine ranking under the modern system was that of a [[Bekkaku Kanpeisha]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. The shrine's crest (kamon) is the "Ho Kagari Bune" of the Nawa clan. [[File:Nawa-jinja Torii.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Torii]] [[File:Nawa-jinja Rōmon.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Rōmon gate]] == Deities == Nawa Shrine enshrines Nawa Nagatoshi as the main deity, along with 42 members of the Nawa clan. Nawa Nagatoshi was a samurai who supported Emperor Go-Daigo in overthrowing the Kamakura shogunate and fought for the Southern Court during the [[Nanboku-chō period]]. * [[Nawa Yoshitaka]] - Eldest son of Nagatoshi. Killed in the Battle of Sakaiura in 1338. * [[Nawa Takamitsu]] - Third son of Nagatoshi. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Nawa Yasunaga - Younger brother of Nagatoshi (third brother). Committed suicide in Izumo Province in 1333. * Nawa Yukitomo - Younger brother of Nagatoshi (ninth brother). Committed suicide in Senjōzan, Hōki Province in 1334. * Nawa Nagashige - Nephew of Nagatoshi. * Nawa Yoshishige - Nephew of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Nagayoshi. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Takamichi - Nephew of Nagashige, second son of Suketaka. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takamasa - Younger brother of Takamichi. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Nagauji - Nephew of Nagatoshi, third son of Yukiuji. Killed in Hachiman in 1340. * Nawa Sadauji - Younger brother of Nagauji. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takakami - Nephew of Nagatoshi, second son of Takashige. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takanaga - Nephew of Nagatoshi, second son of Takakane. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Yukishige - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Yukimura. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Hidemura - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Yorimura. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Gorōbyōe-no-jō - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nagamura, eldest son of Koremura. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Shigemura - Younger brother of Gorōbyōe-no-jō. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Okimura - Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Nobusada - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Yukisada. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Hiromasa - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin Nobusada, second son of Hiromasa. Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Nawa Hirotsugu - Grandson of Nobusada's cousin. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Sukesada - Cousin of Nagatoshi, younger brother of Nobusada. Killed in Nijo Domiya in 1333. * Nawa Sukeshige - Cousin of Nagatoshi, younger brother of Sukesada. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Nagashige - Eldest son of Nagasada. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Takanobu - Eldest son of Takayuki. Killed in Tomioka, Bizen Province in 1343. * Nawa Yukizane - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Yukitada. Killed in Hōki Province in 1342. * Nawa Sukekuni - Cousin of Nagatoshi, eldest son of Takasuke. Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Nawa Takakuni - Grandson of Nagatoshi's cousin, eldest son of Sukekuni. Killed in Sakainan, Echizen Province in 1336. * Uchikawa Masanobu - Retainer of Nagatoshi. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Masachika - Second son of Uchikawa Masanobu. Committed suicide at Mt. Shosha in Harima Province in 1334. * Uchikawa Masakazu - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, eldest son of Nagaaki. Killed in Nijo Domiya in 1333. * Uchikawa Masayuki - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, younger brother of Masakazu. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Yuki - Nephew of Uchikawa Masanobu, younger brother of Masayuki. Killed in Nishisakamoto in 1336. * Uchikawa Yoshihiro - Grandson of Uchikawa Masanobu, fourth son of Yoshimasa. Killed in Higo Province in 1378. * Uchikawa Yukikage - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Uchikawa Takekage - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Uchikawa Kunitoki - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Kawase Yoshimoto - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Kawase Tadayori - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Aramatsu Tadanari - Killed in Sakaiura in 1338. * Kohara Hayashimoto - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Komon Yukikiyo - Killed in Kyoto in 1336. * Tsuchiya Munekiyo - Killed in Hachiman in 1342. == History == The origins of Nawa Shrine trace back to the Shōō and Meireki eras (1652-1658), when local people built a small shrine at the site of Nawa Nagatoshi's residence to honor his virtues. In Enpō 5 (1677), the [[Tottori Domain|Lord of Tottori]], [[Ikeda Mitsunaka]], revered Nagatoshi and built a new shrine at the Hiyoshizaka Sannō Gongen site, naming it Ujidono Gongen. According to the explanations of the early Meiji era, the revival of the shrine was initiated by Mitsunaka's son, [[Ikeda Tsunakiyo]], who allocated a small amount of festival funds each year. During the late Edo period, the domain lord [[Ikeda Yoshitake]] intended to appoint a priest for the shrine but was interrupted by the abolition of feudal domains and the establishment of prefectures. In 1873 (Meiji 7), the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine and renamed Ujidono Shrine. On June 22, 1876 (Meiji 9), Tottori Prefecture requested the Ministry of Home Affairs to elevate Ujidono Shrine to the rank of a national shrine, citing the accomplishments of the Nawa clan comparable to those of the Kusunoki clan. In October, the shrine priest Kusakaya Suee submitted a similar request to the Ministry of Education, and in November, Ikeda Yoshitake petitioned the Imperial Household Ministry for the shrine's elevation. On December 16, Shimane Prefecture, which had incorporated Tottori Prefecture at that time, supported these requests to the Ministry of Education. In the summer of 1877 (Meiji 10), Ikeda Yoshitake visited Ujidono Shrine during a journey from Shimane Prefecture to Tottori and witnessed its dilapidated state. Shortly afterward, Yoshitake passed away, and on November 28, his son, [[Ikeda Terutomo]], petitioned for the shrine's elevation, fulfilling his father's will. The Ministry of Home Affairs had already decided to rank the shrine as a special government shrine, and in response to Terutomo's petition, recommended the implementation to the Dajōkan (Great Council of State). Consequently, on January 10, 1878 (Meiji 11), the Dajōkan decided to rename Ujidono Shrine to Nawa Shrine and rank it as a special government shrine, also enshrining the spirits of Nawa Nagashige and other warriors. In the same year, on May 11, 1878, the Ministry of the Imperial Household decided to set the shrine's festival date to August 15, converting the date of Nagatoshi's death from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. On May 21, the Dajōkan officially established the festival date. Plans for reconstructing the dilapidated shrine buildings were devised by Shimane Prefecture and the Ministry of Home Affairs, with construction permits obtained on January 17, 1879 (Meiji 12), projecting government funding of 4769 yen, 21 sen, and 6 rin over three years. In 1881 (Meiji 14), further external structures and facilities were constructed at government expense. The shrine buildings were completed in 1882 (Meiji 15), and the relocation ceremony was held on March 10, 1883 (Meiji 16), with Tottori Prefectural Governor [[Yamada Nobumichi]] serving as the imperial envoy, presenting a new mirror from the Imperial Household Ministry as the shrine's sacred object. == Timeline == * [[June 22]], 1876 (Meiji 9): Tottori Prefecture requests the Ministry of Home Affairs to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a national shrine. * October 28: Kusakaya Suee requests the Ministry of Education to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a special government shrine. * November: Ikeda Yoshitake requests the Imperial Household Ministry to elevate Ujidono Shrine to a special government shrine. * December 16: Shimane Prefecture supports the requests to the Ministry of Education. * Summer 1877 (Meiji 10): Ikeda Yoshitake visits Ujidono Shrine. * October 20: The Ministry of Home Affairs decides to rank the shrine as a special government shrine. * November 28: Ikeda Terutomo petitions the Imperial Household Ministry to fulfill his father's will. * December 11: The Ministry of Home Affairs submits the petition to the Dajōkan. * [[January 10]], 1878 (Meiji 11): The Dajōkan decides to rename Ujidono Shrine to Nawa Shrine and rank it as a special government shrine. * May 11: The Ministry of the Imperial Household requests the Dajōkan to set the festival date to August 15. * May 21: The Dajōkan establishes the festival date as August 15. * June 19: Shimane Prefecture submits the shrine building reconstruction plan to the Ministry of Home Affairs. * August 31: The Ministry of Home Affairs issues instructions to Shimane Prefecture regarding the shrine building reconstruction plan. * November 15: Shimane Prefecture submits a revised plan with location maps. * December 21: The Ministry of Home Affairs submits a petition to the Dajōkan for the shrine building reconstruction. * December 25: The Dajōkan's investigation bureau recommends the approval of the reconstruction. * [[January 17]], 1879 (Meiji 12): The Dajōkan approves the reconstruction of Nawa Shrine. * October 30, 1879: The Dajōkan determines the order of Nawa Shrine among special government shrines, placing it after Minatogawa Shrine. * [[March 10]], 1883 (Meiji 16): The relocation ceremony. == References == {{Reflist}} === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="Annotations"}} === Sources === Collection)] * Okada Yoneo, "History of the Establishment of Shrines and Shrines", in Matsuyama Noho, ed., "One Hundred Years of Meiji Restoration Shinto History" Vol. 2, Shinto Culture Association, 1966. * "Daijōruiten", viewed in October 2011 at the [https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/ National Archives of Japan Digital Archive]. == Related Literature == *安津素彦・梅田義彦編集兼監修者『神道辞典』神社新報社、1968年、44頁 *白井永二・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、258-259頁== See Also == {{commonscat|Nawa-jinja}} * [[Chōkō-ji Temple]] * [[Tsubota Shrine]] == External Links == * [http://www.daisen.jp/p/2/area/umigawa/30/ Daisen
